user_prompt: implement the stuff I said in `lib\AudioManager\readme.md`
let me know if my strategy doesn't make sense
agent_reply: Implemented `lib/AudioManager` as a namespace module with:
- Mic mute/unmute mapped onto the mic FIFOs
- `i2s_channel_register_event_callback` does not exist in this Arduino ESP32 / ESP-IDF 4.4 setup, so I used the legacy I2S event queue from `i2s_driver_install(...)`.
- Core2 internal mic and speaker are not true full-duplex here. The API supports switching speaker/mic mode, but not simultaneous I2S RX/TX on the internal hardware.
- WM8960 is stubbed with a clear log error because the repo does not yet define its pins/codec setup.
Verification: I forced PlatformIO to compile and link `AudioManager` with a temporary check file, then removed that file. Build passed.

user_prompt: hmmm this slipped by me, AudioManager should not be applying the volume to samples, it should just set the volume on the amplifier or DAC, if you don't have enough data on how to do this then just put in a stub, remove both `mono_to_stereo_with_volume` and `apply_volume`
also, make the volume range handled by AudioManager 0 to 30 so we can handle bluetooth 0 to 15 by steps of 2 and generic 10% increments with steps of 3
agent_reply: AudioManager now uses a `0..30` volume range in [AudioManager.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:28). `volumeUp()`/`volumeDown()` step by `3`, and Bluetooth HFP volume maps `0..15` directly to `0..30` by multiplying by `2`.

I removed sample-volume scaling from [AudioManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:289). The speaker pump now uses `AudioFifo::dequeueStereo()` directly, so samples are duplicated mono-to-stereo but not gain-adjusted. The old `apply_volume` and `mono_to_stereo_with_volume` helpers are gone.
Hardware volume is now an explicit stub: `update_hardware_volume()`. It just logs for now, which keeps the control path in the right place without pretending we know the Core2 amp/DAC gain route yet.
- Built with a temporary BluetoothManager compile hook: passed.
- Removed the hook and ran normal `pio run`: passed.
- Remaining warnings are the existing unrelated third-party / packed-member warnings.

user_prompt: for BluetoothManager, inside the handler for `ESP_HF_CLIENT_AUDIO_STATE_EVT` , I don't see the same handling for `ESP_HF_CLIENT_AUDIO_STATE_CONNECTED`, `ESP_HF_CLIENT_AUDIO_STATE_CONNECTED_MSBC`, I only see the `ESP_HF_CLIENT_AUDIO_STATE_DISCONNECTED` state being handled.

AudioManager needs to accept a setting to know if mSBC decoding is required or not, and if the samples are 8 kHz or 16 kHz so it can pick the right way to queue into the FIFO. BluetoothManager needs to use this feature.
agent_reply: BluetoothManager now handles all three HFP audio states in [BluetoothManager.cpp](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BluetoothManager.cpp:250):
- `ESP_HF_CLIENT_AUDIO_STATE_DISCONNECTED` -> resets to CVSD/8 kHz before reconnect attempt
AudioManager now has a public HFP audio format setting in [AudioManager.h](d:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:26):
And the callbacks now use that setting:
- Incoming CVSD queues PCM as 8 kHz so `AudioFifo` upsamples.
- Incoming mSBC decodes with `libsbc`, then queues decoded 16 kHz PCM.
- Outgoing CVSD downsamples internal 16 kHz mic FIFO to 8 kHz.
- Outgoing mSBC encodes 16 kHz PCM with `libsbc`.
- Built with temporary BluetoothManager compile hook: passed.
- Removed hook and ran normal `pio run`: passed.
- `clang-format` was not available on PATH, so I did not run formatting. Existing unrelated warnings remain.

user_prompt: AudioManager needs to track the mic level simply to show a peaking mic level meter

Inside `pump_mic2bt`, if `i2s_channel_read` is successful, we know how long the chunk is, 16000 samples is one second, and +/- 32767 the maximum possible value. Track the peak value using the absolute value of every sample. Decay it by the appropriate amount such that the decay takes 3 seconds to reach zero. The decay amount is thus calculated from the `bytes_read` variable and also which kind of mic is being used. But also subtract 1 even if nothing is read.
While the peak is tracked using 16 bits, provide a getter method that returns 0 to 100, where 100 is only used to indicate possible clipping.
agent_reply: - `pump_mic2bt()` now updates a 16-bit mic peak whenever `i2s_channel_read()` succeeds.
- SPM1423 uses mono sample count as duration.
- WM8960 uses stereo frame count as duration, while still scanning every sample for peak.
- Peak decays over roughly 3 seconds based on chunk duration.
- If no audio is read, it still decays by `1`.
- Added `AudioManager::micPeakLevel()` in [AudioManager.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h), returning `0..100`; `100` only happens at full-scale `32767`.
I tried `pio run`, but `pio` is still not available on PATH in this shell.
- `pump_mic2bt()` now updates a 16-bit mic peak whenever `i2s_channel_read()` succeeds.
- SPM1423 uses mono sample count as duration.
- WM8960 uses stereo frame count as duration, while still scanning every sample for peak.
- Peak decays over roughly 3 seconds based on chunk duration.
- If no audio is read, it still decays by `1`.
- Added `AudioManager::micPeakLevel()` in [AudioManager.h](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h), returning `0..100`; `100` only happens at full-scale `32767`.

user_prompt: I removed references to WM8960 because it is obsolete, new hardware target is SGTL5000 but I renamed a ton of stuff as external I2S codec
agent_reply: If WM8960 is obsolete, this dependency should be removed or replaced with the SGTL5000/control library you actually plan to use. Right now the build still pulls WM8960-specific code.
That conflicts with the new direction. If WM8960 is no longer valid hardware, this doc should say only SGTL5000 or generic external I2S codec.

Both `enable_exti2scodec_speaker()` and `enable_exti2scodec_mic()` return `false`, so selecting `Hardware::ExternalI2SCodec` cannot currently enable speaker or mic mode. If the rename was meant to preserve behavior while swapping codec hardware, this is the main functional gap.
4. [AudioManager.cpp](c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:553) assumes every external I2S codec mic stream is stereo.

That matches what you described, but it should be considered part of the external codec contract. If SGTL5000 is ever configured mono at the codec or I2S slot level, this path will incorrectly halve the frame count and mix adjacent mono samples as L/R.

The broad rename to `ExternalI2SCodec` looks consistent in `AudioManager.h/.cpp`; I don’t see leftover `WM8960` identifiers in that module. The stale references are mainly dependency/docs, plus the external codec setup stubs. I couldn’t use `git diff` because this shell reported the directory as not a git repo.

user_prompt: I want to develop a microphone gain manager, using `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.cpp` and `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.h`. There only needs to be one instance. The microhpone stream is assumed to be mono. The bit depth is always 16 bit PCM just like the rest of the project. The sample rate does not matter.

There should be a public facing initialization function and a "process" function that acts in-place on a buffer. There is a privately kept set of state variables. Also publically there are some getters and setters as needed.
All functionality related to tracking mic peak will now be moved to `MicGainManager`
The initialization will set an initial mic gain of 1.5x, note that a test showed that actually screaming into the mic results in about 75% full scale peak.

There is a target-gain, a current-gain, and a previous-gain. The current-gain can go up or go down once per call of process. The rate it goes up is slower than the rate it can go down (it's more important to respond to clipping).

The difference between current-gain and previous-gain is that, at the begining of "process", it uses previous-gain until a zero-cross event, then it uses current-gain and updates previous-gain. And then calculate the current-gain according to target-gain.
The mic peak is tracked using the raw unmodified values. The peak being tracked decays using the same method we use now, but called from within "process".
The target-gain is continously set to keep the mic peak at about 75% of full scale. It is clamped between 1x and 3x.
The mic peak is publicly readable so the GUI can display the peak, the mic peak after scaling with the current setting is also publicly available.
There is also to be a fixed gain mode, for when users manually control the gain. And there is to be a bypass mode.

The way the sample manipulation should work is: cast the sample to 32 bit, the 32 bit sample is multiplied by a gain, and then divided by a divisor, then written back into the original buffer as 16 bit. The gain scale is thus controlled by the divisor. We can start with a divisor of 128, and so the gain step of 1 is achievable and if my calculations are correct:

Sample rate is 16000 Hz, the I2S read buffer is set to 240 samples, so there are 66.67 calls of "process" per second. If we use 128 as the divisor, and want to double the gain automatically as slow as possible, we can go from 128 to 256 in about 2 seconds. And using 128, a power of 2, gives the compiler a chance to use bit shifting for the division. And 128 is decent resolution such that humans cannot detect any jarring transitions.
Warn me if my logic is flawed.
agent_reply: - Implemented in-place 16-bit mono PCM processing in [MicGainManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/MicGainManager.cpp:184).
- Moved mic peak tracking out of `AudioManager`.
- Added auto gain, fixed gain mode, bypass mode, raw peak, and scaled peak accessors.
- Wired `AudioManager::pump_mic2bt()` to process mic samples before queueing them in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:643).
Your divisor math is good: `240` samples at `16000 Hz` is `66.67` process calls/sec, so `128` one-unit gain steps takes about `1.92s`.
- Initial `1.5x` gain means a raw `75%` full-scale scream can clip at the output before AGC pulls down.
- During real active silence, auto gain will drift toward `3x`; that can make the first loud transient hot. I prevented no-sample/idling calls from ramping gain.
- Zero-cross-only gain switching can delay gain changes if the mic signal has DC offset or very few crossings. A timeout fallback may be worth adding later.
Small note: `pio run` regenerated the auto-generated `src/version.c` build nonce.

- Wired `AudioManager::pump_mic2bt()` to process mic samples before queueing them in [AudioManager.cpp](/c:/GitHubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:643).
Your divisor math is good: `240` samples at `16000 Hz` is `66.67` process calls/sec, so `128` one-unit gain steps takes about `1.92s`.

I added `AudioManager::HfpAudioDiagnostics` plus snapshot/reset APIs in [AudioManager.h](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.h:33), and wired counters through HFP incoming/outgoing audio plus speaker I2S writes in [AudioManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioManager/AudioManager.cpp:718).

The speakerphone test now resets those counters before the pump loop and logs them every 2 seconds with `ESP_LOGI` from [test_btspeakerphone.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/tests/test_btspeakerphone.cpp:75). You’ll get three lines: incoming decode/FIFO stats, outgoing mic stats, and speaker I2S/FIFO state.

commit_message: speakerphone test works, it did involve editing the esp-idf config and building the library in the back

user_prompt: that didn't fix it, still 0 callbacks, add diagnostic prints for `audio diag mic` so we also count I2S reads and queued samples, enough information to know if we are meeting the conditions for `notifyOutgoingAudioReady`
agent_reply: Added `audio diag mic` logging and the underlying counters.
- mic pump calls and skip reasons: not mic mode / no I2S / FIFO full
- I2S read calls, requested/read bytes, empty reads, read errors, raw samples
- notify condition checks, ready count, actual notify calls
- failure buckets for notify: no queued data / below minimum / Bluetooth not ready
- current mic FIFO readable samples, notify minimum, and current `can_bt`
On the next log, the key read is:
If `call` increments but `audio diag out cb` stays `0`, then we are notifying Bluedroid but it still is not fetching outgoing audio.

user_prompt: apply the fix for the full fifo case, but also, letting the mic2bt fifo fill up is a problem. Queuing mic2file should be allowed but queuing mic2bt should be choked unless we are certain that Bluetooth audio is ready to push into
agent_reply: - `mic2file` keeps getting queued whenever mic samples are read.
- `mic2bt` is cleared and not queued while `BtManager::canNotifyOutgoingAudioReady()` is false.
- If Bluetooth is ready but `mic2bt` is full, `pump_mic2bt()` now attempts `notifyOutgoingAudioReady()` instead of returning early.
- `mic2bt` only receives new mic samples when Bluetooth is ready and the FIFO has write space.
- `bt_drop notready/full=.../...` was added to `audio diag mic`.
- `notready` counts mic samples intentionally not queued to Bluetooth because HFP audio was not ready.
- `full` counts mic samples that could not fit into `mic2bt` while Bluetooth was ready.
In the next log, the important thing is whether `q_bt` starts increasing only after `can_bt=1`, and whether `notify ... call` increments.
- `mic2file` keeps getting queued whenever mic samples are read.
- `mic2bt` is cleared and not queued while `BtManager::canNotifyOutgoingAudioReady()` is false.
- If Bluetooth is ready but `mic2bt` is full, `pump_mic2bt()` now attempts `notifyOutgoingAudioReady()` instead of returning early.
- `mic2bt` only receives new mic samples when Bluetooth is ready and the FIFO has write space.
- `bt_drop notready/full=.../...` was added to `audio diag mic`.
- `notready` counts mic samples intentionally not queued to Bluetooth because HFP audio was not ready.
- `full` counts mic samples that could not fit into `mic2bt` while Bluetooth was ready.
In the next log, the important thing is whether `q_bt` starts increasing only after `can_bt=1`, and whether `notify ... call` increments.

user_prompt: implement in `lib\AudioManager\SpeakerPeakActivity.cpp` and `lib\AudioManager\SpeakerPeakActivity.h` a peak level tracker that works exactly like the one used for mic peak level in `lib\AudioManager\MicGainManager.cpp`, have it analyze samples as they arrive from the bluetooth callback so that they are raw when they are analyzed
agent_reply: - It mirrors the mic peak behavior: signed 16-bit PCM absolute peak, decay over time, `rawPeak()` and `rawPeakLevel()`.
- `AudioManager::hfp_incoming_audio()` now analyzes incoming Bluetooth PCM before it enters the FIFOs, so it is still raw callback audio.
- Added `AudioManager::speakerPeakLevel()` next to the existing mic peak getters.
Build passed. `src/version.c` was regenerated by the build.

user_prompt: please move the functionality of `bool setSpeakerMuted(bool muted)` into `lib\AudioManager\AudioManager.cpp`
agent_reply: The old `RecordingViewCallbacks::setSpeakerMuted()` / toggle / getter remain as thin wrappers, so the view code doesn’t need to know about `AudioManager` directly.
I also made `AudioManager::setVolume()` respect the muted state: if speaker is muted, a new nonzero volume is remembered as the restore volume while actual output stays at `kMinVolume`.
Build passes. `src/version.c` was regenerated by the build.
